4	  depends_on:
5	    - helix.prd
6	---
7	# Feature: DDx Agent Service (consumer of ddx-agent contract)
8	
9	**ID:** FEAT-006
10	**Status:** In Progress (migrating to ddx-agent CONTRACT-003)
11	**Priority:** P0
12	**Owner:** DDx Team
13	
14	## Overview
15	
16	DDx invokes LLMs through the `ddx-agent` module, defined by
17	**CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service** in the `~/Projects/agent` repo
18	(`docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md`).
19	
20	That contract is the entire execution boundary. DDx exposes the public
21	`ddx run` / `ddx try` / `ddx work` layers and calls the upstream
22	`agentlib.DdxAgent` interface for the actual agent invocation. Upstream
23	diagnostic/status commands may remain mounted under `ddx agent`, but
24	`ddx agent run`, `ddx agent execute-bead`, and `ddx agent execute-loop` are not
25	public workflow commands.
26	
27	## DDx-side responsibilities
28	
29	DDx owns the bead-driven workflow surface. It does NOT own:
30	- Harness orchestration (claude, codex, opencode, pi, gemini, native)
31	- Provider routing
32	- Model catalog
33	- Compaction
34	- Tool registration
35	- Session-log shape
36	
37	Those all live inside ddx-agent per CONTRACT-003.
38	
39	DDx owns:
40	
41	- **Bead-driven invocation.** `ddx try`, `ddx work`, and the server's
42	  queue-drain worker translate bead state into `ExecuteRequest` values and
43	  surface results back into the bead tracker. The queue-drain worker
44	  defaults to a long-running 30s `--poll-interval` (ddx-dc157075):
45	  workers stay alive across empty polls and surface "no ready work" as a
46	  transient `idle` substate. Operators opt out with `--once` (one-shot)
47	  or `--poll-interval=0` (legacy drain-and-exit).
48	- **Bead-attempt orchestration.** Worktree creation, base-revision pinning,
49	  result landing (merge / preserve / no-changes), gate evaluation, evidence
50	  bundle capture. The agent provides the LLM execution; DDx provides the
51	  git-aware orchestration.
52	- **Evidence and session capture.** DDx writes `.ddx/executions/<id>/`
53	  bundles with prompts, manifests, and result artifacts. The agent's session
54	  log path (returned in `ExecuteResponse.SessionLogPath`) is captured into
55	  the bundle.
56	- **Power policy at the request level.** DDx selects requested agent power
57	  bounds, effort, and permissions per bead attempt based on bead metadata, user
58	  input, and prior attempt outcomes. The agent receives `MinPower` and
59	  optionally `MaxPower` on `ExecuteRequest` and performs the routing.
60	- **Agent passthrough constraints.** DDx may accept explicit `--harness`,
61	  `--provider`, and `--model` values from the operator and pass them unchanged
62	  to the agent. DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, rewrite, or reason about
63	  these fields; they are opaque constraints for the agent to interpret.
64	- **Retry policy.** DDx owns the bead retry loop because DDx owns the evidence
65	  needed to decide whether an attempt succeeded: commits, merge/preserve
66	  result, no-changes rationale, post-run gates, review verdicts, cooldowns,
67	  and prior run metadata. On retry, DDx may raise `MinPower`. The agent owns
68	  how those power bounds map to a concrete model/provider.
69	
70	## Power Intent
71	
72	DDx does not route. DDx chooses request-level power bounds and sends them to
73	`agentlib.DdxAgent.Execute` as `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`; the agent
74	resolves harness, provider, endpoint, model, health, quota, fallback, and route
75	errors.
76	
77	Power is an abstract integer scale owned by the agent contract. DDx treats
78	`MinPower`/`MaxPower` as bounds on that scale, not as model identities. For
79	example, the agent may report:
80	
81	```text
82	running with qwen 3.6-27b (power 10)
83	```
84	
85	DDx records requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower` and the actual model/power returned
86	by the agent. DDx can use that evidence on a later retry to raise `MinPower`,
87	but it still does not choose the next model.
88	
89	The agent also exposes its available model/power catalog. DDx may read that
90	catalog to choose a `MinPower` threshold for "top model only" retries, for
91	example by requesting a lower bound at or above the lowest power among the
92	current top models. DDx must not use the catalog to pin a concrete
93	model/provider on the normal execution path.
94	
95	DDx may also pass request facts such as estimated prompt size, whether tools are
96	required, permissions, timeout values, and effort/reasoning intent. These facts
97	describe the work; they do not select a model.
98	
99	## Non-Bead Invocation Intent
100	
101	Not every layer-1 invocation comes from a bead. Artifact generation,
102	artifact regeneration, prompt comparison skills, and diagnostics can call
103	`ddx run` directly. For those calls DDx may choose work intent from the
104	artifact or operation being performed:
105	
106	- `MinPower` / optional `MaxPower` derived from the artifact's generator,
107	  media type, size, risk, or explicit operator flags
108	- permissions needed for the operation, such as read-only, workspace-write, or
109	  network access
110	- timeout and effort/reasoning intent
111	- metadata such as `produces_artifact`, artifact id, execution definition id,
112	  and governing document ids
113	
114	These are DDx-owned work facts, not routing decisions. DDx must not translate an
115	artifact key, media type, generator name, or execution definition into a concrete
116	harness/provider/model. The only concrete route-like fields DDx may send are
117	operator-supplied passthrough constraints, carried unchanged in the passthrough
118	envelope described below.
119	
120	## Agent Passthrough Constraints
121	
122	`--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are permitted on `ddx run`, `ddx try`,
123	and `ddx work` only as passthrough fields. DDx carries them in one narrow
124	request envelope and sends them to `agentlib.DdxAgent.Execute` unchanged.
125	
126	These fields must not leak into DDx routing policy:
127	
128	- no DDx-side validation against harness/provider/model catalogs
129	- no preflight `ResolveRoute`
130	- no provider/model fallback or substitution
131	- no retry-policy branches that inspect the string values
132	- no config-driven default harness/provider/model selection
133	- no worker columns or queue selection logic that treat these as DDx concepts
134	
135	If an operator supplies passthrough constraints and DDx later retries with a
136	higher `MinPower`, DDx keeps the passthrough values unchanged. The agent
137	decides whether the power bounds and passthrough constraints are compatible and
138	reports the actual model/power or a typed error. DDx records the requested
139	passthrough values for audit, but it does not use them to select a route.
140	
141	When hard passthrough pins make the requested power bounds unsatisfiable, DDx
142	must stop with a typed terminal classification such as
143	`blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or `agent_power_unsatisfied`. DDx records
144	the requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower`, passthrough envelope, and agent-supplied
145	evidence, then reports operator action required. DDx must not remove pins,
146	choose alternatives, call `ResolveRoute` to work around the conflict, or loop on
147	higher `MinPower` values.
148	
149	The only DDx code allowed to touch raw passthrough fields is the CLI parser, the
150	single passthrough/intent value, Execute request construction, execution-record
151	persistence, and human-facing evidence rendering. Retry, queue selection, work
152	scheduling, catalog threshold helpers, and config defaulting must carry the
153	opaque value without reading `Harness`, `Provider`, or `Model`.
154	
155	`ResolveRoute` is not part of normal execution. It is allowed only for
156	operator-facing status/debug surfaces such as route-status and provider
157	diagnostics. A `RouteDecision` returned by `ResolveRoute` must never be
158	re-injected into `Execute`.
159	
160	`agent.routing.profile_ladders`, `agent.routing.model_overrides`,
161	`agent.routing.profile_priority`, and the `cheap`/`standard`/`smart` profile
162	taxonomy are not DDx execution policy. Configs or tests that rely on DDx-managed
163	ladders, profiles, or model overrides are migration debt.
164	
165	## Catalog and Status Boundary
166	
167	Model/power catalog rendering is observability. Catalog status surfaces may show
168	model names, providers, powers, and route candidates returned by the agent for
169	debugging. Retry threshold policy may consume only abstract power numbers from
170	the catalog to compute `MinPower` thresholds. Neither status nor retry code may
171	return or mutate concrete harness/provider/model pins for execution.
172	
173	## Session Log Envelope Boundary
174	
175	The upstream agent owns the native session-log schema and any harness-specific
176	details inside it. DDx owns only the envelope around that log:
177	
178	- request id, layer, parent run id, bead id or produced artifact id
179	- requested `MinPower` / `MaxPower` and opaque passthrough constraints
180	- actual model and actual power returned by the agent
181	- pointer or copied attachment for `ExecuteResponse.SessionLogPath`
182	- DDx-owned attempt outcome, merge/preserve outcome, gates, and evidence refs
183	
184	DDx may render or link the session log for humans, but normal execution policy
185	must not parse the inner session log to infer routing, provider health, model
186	fallbacks, or retry destinations. Typed agent response fields and DDx-owned
187	attempt evidence are the policy inputs.
188	
189	## Layer Ownership Migration Table
190	
191	FEAT-006 is deliberately narrow after the three-layer refactor. It describes
192	how DDx consumes the upstream agent contract and how layer-1 requests are
193	formed; it does not own worktree, queue, or CLI namespace design.
194	
195	| Area | Owner after migration |
196	| --- | --- |
197	| CONTRACT-003 `agentlib.DdxAgent` boundary, `ExecuteRequest` / `ExecuteResponse` consumption, `MinPower` / `MaxPower`, actual model/power recording | Stays in FEAT-006 |
198	| Opaque passthrough envelope for `--harness`, `--provider`, `--model` and the rule that DDx must not route on those fields | Stays in FEAT-006 |
199	| Non-bead layer-1 invocation intent: artifact-keyed power bounds, permissions, timeout, effort, and metadata | Stays in FEAT-006 |
200	| Session-log envelope and pointer/copy capture around the agent-owned inner log | Stays in FEAT-006 |
201	| Worktree creation, base revision pinning, bead prompt resolution, result landing, preserve refs, post-run gates, evidence bundles | Moves to FEAT-010 layer 2 (`ddx try`) |
202	| Unified `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` substrate, layer metadata, child/parent run links, migration from `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/` | Moves to FEAT-010 |
203	| Queue iteration, no-progress detection, retry scheduling, cooldowns, stop conditions, loop records | Moves to FEAT-010 layer 3 (`ddx work`) |
204	| Public top-level command shape: `ddx run`, `ddx try`, `ddx work`, `ddx runs`, `ddx tries`, `ddx work workers` | Moves to FEAT-001 |
205	| Structural mounting of the upstream agent Cobra root under `ddx agent` and hard-deprecation redirects for legacy DDx workflow commands | Moves to FEAT-001, with the contract boundary referenced here |
206	
207	### Conflict-recovery structured outcomes (bead ddx-0097af14)
208	
209	Before parking a bead on `land_conflict` (or on `execution_failed` when the
210	agent produced a commit before failing), the `ddx work` drain now attempts to reuse
211	the preserved iteration ref rather than discarding the agent's work:
212	
213	1. **3-way ort auto-merge** (`git merge --no-ff -s ort -X ours`). If clean,
214	   the bead closes as `success`.
215	2. **Focused conflict-resolve agent** (if `ConflictResolver` is configured on
216	   the worker). If clean, the bead closes as `success`.
217	3. **Park with structured outcome.** Two new statuses replace the generic
218	   reopen when both recovery paths fail:
219	
220	| Status | Event kind | Meaning |
221	| --- | --- | --- |
222	| `land_conflict_unresolvable` | `land-conflict-unresolvable` | All auto-recovery failed; retryable after 15-min cooldown. |
223	| `land_conflict_needs_human` | `land-conflict-needs-human` | Focused-resolve agent returned BLOCKING; human input required. |
224	
225	Both statuses use `LandConflictCooldown` (15 min) rather than the 24h cap used
226	for `push_failed`, because land conflicts typically unblock quickly as sibling
227	beads advance the base branch.
228	
229	### Disrupted vs no-progress cooldown policy (bead ddx-5b3e57f4)
230	
231	The execute-loop cooldown branch must distinguish *worker disruption* from
232	*model gave up*. A failed attempt where the model never had a chance to commit
233	— context cancelled, executor killed by SIGTERM/SIGKILL, transport-class
234	provider error, server restart, routing preflight rejection — is NOT evidence
235	the model could not make progress on the bead. Parking such an attempt under
236	the 6h `noProgressCooldown` silently freezes important work whenever a worker
237	is disrupted by an unrelated cause.
238	
239	The loop classifies the failure via `classifyDisruption(ctx, executorErr)` and
240	sets `ExecuteBeadReport.Disrupted = true` plus a stable `DisruptionReason`
241	kind:
242	
243	| Reason | Trigger |
244	| --- | --- |
245	| `context_canceled` | `ctx.Err() == context.Canceled` (SIGINT, parent died, Stop()) |
246	| `context_deadline` | `ctx.Err() == context.DeadlineExceeded` |
247	| `transport_error` | Executor error matches a transport-class marker (connection refused/reset, gateway, EOF, TLS handshake, …) |
248	| `preflight_rejected` | Upstream `RoutePreflight` rejected the (harness, model) pair |
249	
250	When `report.Disrupted` is true:
251	
252	1. `shouldSuppressNoProgress` returns false unconditionally — the loop
253	   bypasses `SetExecutionCooldown` so the bead is immediately re-claimable.
254	2. A `disruption_detected` event is appended to the bead and emitted on the
255	   loop event sink (`{reason, detail, harness, model}`) so operators can see
256	   disruption rates and which class is firing.
257	3. Genuine no-progress (clean executor return, BaseRev == ResultRev, not
258	   Disrupted) still hits the configured `noProgressCooldown` (default 6h).
259	
260	The classifier is intentionally fuzzy on transport markers: a false positive
261	costs one quick retry; a false negative costs a 6-hour park for what was a
262	transient outage.
263	
264	## Bead-Attempt Worker Sub-task Discovery Policy
265	
266	**Design position: option (b) — surface via result.**
267	
268	Workers executing inside `ddx try` / `ddx work` bead attempts MUST NOT call
269	`ddx bead create`
270	in-band during their execution run. Creating beads in-band (option c) allows a
271	single worker to flood the queue with unreviewed children — in one observed case
272	(ddx-44236615), one worker spawned 11 P0 children without operator review. This
273	violates the architectural principle from FEAT-013: *"DDx provides primitives,
274	not orchestration. Orchestration policy stays in HELIX and other workflow tools."*
275	
276	Workers MAY surface discovered sub-tasks as structured data in `result.json`
277	under a `discovered_subtasks` array. Each entry is a lightweight object with at
278	minimum a `title` and optional `description`, `labels`, and `priority` fields.
279	The `ddx work` drain or supervisor reads this array from the result bundle and passes
280	it to the workflow tool (HELIX or operator) for decomposition decisions. The
281	supervisor decides whether, when, and at what priority to file new beads — DDx
282	does not do this automatically.
283	
284	### Rationale
285	
286	| Option | Description | Problem |
287	| --- | --- | --- |
288	| (a) Append to parent | Worker writes sub-tasks as notes or events on the parent bead | Loses structure; sub-tasks are not queryable or executable without manual decomposition |
289	| (b) Surface via result (**chosen**) | Agent emits `discovered_subtasks` in result.json; supervisor decides | Gates tracker mutations at an explicit decision point; aligns with "primitives not orchestration" |
290	| (c) Create in-band (former behavior) | Worker calls `ddx bead create` directly | Unreviewed children flood the queue; P0 tagging without operator review; violates workflow ownership boundary |
291	
292	Option (b) preserves the operator control point required by workflow tools while
293	providing structure that automation can act on. It is the only option consistent
294	with the FEAT-013 boundary.
295	
296	### Result schema extension
297	
298	`result.json` gains an optional `discovered_subtasks` array. The bead-attempt
299	orchestrator writes it from the agent's structured output when present. The
300	supervisor MUST NOT act on this field automatically; it is surfaced as an
301	observation for the workflow tool.
302	
303	```json
304	{
305	  "status": "success",
306	  "detail": "...",
307	  "discovered_subtasks": [
308	    {
309	      "title": "Add read-coverage for X",
310	      "description": "Optional detail about what is needed",
311	      "labels": ["area:tests"],
312	      "priority": "P1"
313	    }
314	  ]
315	}
316	```
317	
318	If the agent does not emit `discovered_subtasks`, the field is absent. The
319	`ddx work` drain treats an absent or empty array identically — no automatic bead
320	creation occurs in either case.
321	
322	### Enforcement
323	
324	The bead-attempt prompt delivered to the agent (via the prompt rationalizer)
325	MUST include an explicit instruction that workers must not call `ddx bead create`
326	and must instead emit `discovered_subtasks` in their result if sub-tasks are
327	identified. This constraint is part of the prompt contract; it is not enforced
328	by the CLI at runtime.
329	
330	## Malformed-Bead Remediation Decision (beads ddx-7eab13a6 era)
331	
332	**Decision: close and refile.**
333	
334	The layer-1 worktree-path bug fixed in ddx-7eab13a6 caused 22 beads to be
335	created with IDs of the form `.execute-bead-wt-<parent>-<timestamp>-<random>-<hex>`
336	(the worktree directory path was mistakenly used as the bead ID). These beads
337	are invalid: their IDs do not match the `ddx-<8hex>` format expected by all DDx
338	CLI commands, and the IDs appear in git commit trailers as malformed values.
339	
340	Repair in-place is not safe. Bead IDs are immutable identifiers embedded in git
341	commit trailers (`Ddx-Attempt-Id` etc.), inter-bead dependency edges, and the
342	JSONL append-only store. Renaming an existing bead ID requires rewriting
343	history, which violates the merge policy recorded in project memory.
344	
345	The operator (human or supervising workflow tool) should:
346	1. Inspect each malformed bead's description to determine whether the underlying
347	   work is still valid.
348	2. If valid: refile a new bead with the same title and description, using
349	   `ddx bead create`. The new bead will receive a correct ID.
350	3. Close the malformed bead with `ddx bead close <id> --reason "malformed-id
351	   refiled as <new-id>"`.
352	4. If the work is no longer needed: close the malformed bead with reason
353	   `malformed-id obsolete`.
354	
355	No automated bulk remediation is performed. The 22 beads are left open pending
356	operator triage. A future `ddx bead validate` command (not yet implemented) can
357	flag structurally invalid bead IDs to aid discovery.
358	
359	## Asking ddx-agent for changes
360	
361	When DDx needs new behavior from the agent — a new method, a new field on
362	`ExecuteRequest`, a new event type, a new policy knob — file a PR against
363	CONTRACT-003 in the agent repo. Maintainers decide whether the surface grows.
364	
365	Do not import agent internal packages. They live under `internal/` and the
366	Go compiler blocks external imports after agent v0.5.0 ships.
367	
368	### Operator-prompt bead type (Story 15)
369	
370	Free-form prompts submitted from the web UI are persisted as beads with
371	`issueType: operator-prompt` and drained by the standard execute-loop. The
372	title is the prompt's first line, the body is the full prompt verbatim,
373	default labels are `kind:operator-prompt,source:web-ui`, and the AC section
374	is auto-generated from a template. These beads run with the same
375	harness/profile/tier as regular beads but skip the structural AC check
376	(which assumes pre-authored AC). They cannot mutate other operator-prompt
377	beads — a hard rule enforced at the bead store, not at planning time.
378	
379	The trust contract, audit-as-bead model, multi-node delegation policy,
380	prompt-injection threat model, and allowed-mutation scope are captured in
381	**ADR-021: Operator-Prompt Beads as the Web Write Path**.
382	
383	## Worker Contract (per ADR-022)
384	
385	Workers are **long-lived API clients of the server**, not forked
386	subprocesses with private state. The same client/state-machine code path
387	serves both `ddx work --local` (with an in-process API implementation) and
388	server-spawned workers (talking to the HTTP API), parameterised by a
389	`Transport` interface.
390	
391	A worker's lifecycle is fully described by six endpoints under
392	`/api/workers/`:
393	
394	- `POST /register` — worker presents its `ExecuteLoopSpec`-shaped
395	  registration payload (project root, harness, model preference, label
396	  filter, capabilities); receives a `worker_id` and a project-bound
397	  `session_token`. The registration payload is the **single source of
398	  truth** for worker configuration; adding a new flag to `ddx work` means
399	  adding one field here.
400	- `POST /<id>/heartbeat` — mutual liveness signal; carries the worker's
401	  state (`idle | claiming | executing | reviewing | draining`); response
402	  carries a `server_command` (`continue | pause | drain | terminate`).
403	- `GET /<id>/next-bead` — long-poll claim acquisition. Server picks an
404	  eligible bead, creates a claim, and returns `{bead, attempt_id,
405	  base_rev, claim_lease_ms}`. Workers that see a bare `{wait_for_seconds}`
406	  reissue without exiting; empty queue is **not** a termination signal.
407	- `POST /<id>/event` — appends to the bead's event log; uses the existing
408	  bead event `kind`/`body` shape so CLI, MCP, and web-UI readers see
409	  worker events without code changes.
410	- `POST /<id>/result` — terminal disposition for an attempt
411	  (`merged | preserved | no_changes | failed_rejected`), with evidence
412	  directory and commit SHA. Reconciles against the server-side claim.
413	- `POST /<id>/disconnect` — graceful shutdown; releases unclaimed lease.
414	
415	All endpoints require `requireTrusted` (per ADR-006). Session tokens are
416	project-bound; a worker registered for project A cannot read or write
417	state for project B even if it possesses a valid token, which prevents
418	recurrence of the `ddx-4c51d33e` cross-project leak class in worker paths.
419	
420	**Restart survival** is a property of the design, not a feature:
421	
422	- If the server restarts, the worker's heartbeat fails. The worker keeps
423	  executing the in-flight bead in its isolated worktree, marks itself as
424	  disconnected, and reconnects when the server returns. Late results
425	  arriving after a heartbeat-timeout reclaim are recorded as dropped
426	  attempts; the new claim's outcome wins.
427	- If the worker dies, the server's heartbeat-timeout reclaims the bead
428	  for another worker after `3× heartbeat_interval`.
429	
430	The attempt-orchestration responsibilities listed under "DDx-side
431	responsibilities" above (worktree creation, base-revision pinning, result
432	landing, evidence capture) all execute **inside the worker process**,
433	between `next-bead` and `result`. The server holds runtime claim and
434	heartbeat state but does not run agents itself.
435	
436	The legacy bipartite execution paths — `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go`
437	and the server's exec-spawn path in `cli/internal/server/workers.go` — are
438	both replaced by the single worker client + state machine in
439	`cli/internal/agent/work/`. See ADR-022 for the full contract, sequence
440	diagrams, compatibility analysis, and implementation roadmap.
441	
442	## Migration status
443	
444	DDx is currently migrating from the legacy DDx-internal routing/harness
445	implementation to the CONTRACT-003 surface. Migration epic: `ddx-ac5c7fdb`.
446	Pre-migration, FEAT-006 documented harness orchestration responsibilities
447	that have since moved upstream.

52	**Rev 4 design pivot (per user direction 2026-05-03):**
53	"Workers should always be autonomous. If they have a server to talk to,
54	they send results there. They should be able to do their own work and
55	store their logs and artifacts locally without regard for the server.
56	The server is strictly a value-added coordinator."
57	
58	This shifts the decision from Proposal A (server orchestrates; --local has
59	in-process API impl) to a Proposal B-shaped design: workers are always
60	autonomous against the bead store; server is an optional reporting target
61	that provides centralized observability, cross-worker visibility, and UI
62	surfaces — but is NOT required for correct operation. `--local` becomes
63	"force standalone mode; don't try to reach a server" — the same code
64	path that workers take when no server is reachable.
65	
66	Rev 4 removes most of rev 3's machinery as no longer needed:
67	- No authoritative worker registration, heartbeat, or claim-lease API
68	  (there IS a thin identity-envelope POST for correlation; workers
69	  claim from the bead store atomically as today)
70	- No long-poll `next-bead` endpoint (workers pick from the bead store)
71	- No server-side picker (worker-side picker continues, with the
72	  diagnostic events from commit `80f51574` preserved)
73	- No server-side runtime registry as authoritative state (server's view
74	  is derived from worker event reports — eventually-consistent best-effort)
75	- No claim reconciliation across server restarts (server has no claims
76	  to reconcile)
77	- No partition recovery semantics (worker autonomy makes partition
78	  irrelevant)
79	- No data migration story (per user: delete history if needed; clean
80	  slate is fine)

116	  load)

119	  C5/C7 unfreeze at Gate 2, C9 unfreezes at Gate 3

132	**Layout note:** The bead description suggested `docs/helix/03-decide/`. The
133	HELIX layout in this repo places ADRs at `docs/helix/02-design/adr/`. This ADR
134	is filed in the actual location to keep the index discoverable; the next ADR

136	
137	## Context
138	
139	Operators have observed a structural failure mode: when `ddx-server`
140	restarts, in-flight worker subprocesses lose their connection to the
141	orchestrator. Today there are two execution paths that diverge in lifecycle,
142	configuration plumbing, and observability:
143	
144	- **`--local` path.** `ddx work` (a.k.a. `ddx agent execute-loop`) runs an
145	  in-process drain loop that talks to the bead store directly
146	  (`cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go`).
147	- **Server-spawned path.** The server forks a worker process, hand-marshals
148	  an `ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec` over an internal contract, and tracks the PID
149	  in `.ddx/workers/`. The worker reaches back into the bead store directly,
150	  not through the server.
151	
152	Three open beads diagnose the symptom-space:
153	
154	- `ddx-29058e2a` — five flags silently dropped on the server path because
155	  the spec is hand-maintained as parallel structs across six layers.
156	- `ddx-5cb6e6cd` — refactor epic that splits `execute_bead_loop` into
157	  `run/`, `try/`, `work/` packages, exposing an implicit state machine
158	  inside a 700-line function.
159	- `ddx-dc157075` — workers exit prematurely on an empty queue because
160	  `--poll-interval=0` is the default and the per-Run `attempted` map is
161	  scoped to one drain pass.
162	
163	`ddx-4c51d33e` (closed) demonstrated the related security problem: any
164	worker that touches the bead store directly has to reimplement project
165	scoping; the GraphQL resolver got that wrong for `DocumentByPath`.
166	
167	The common cause: **the worker is coupled to local in-process state and
168	the server has no first-class concept of a worker beyond a PID it forked.**
169	Restart loses the in-flight context because the context lives nowhere
170	addressable. Each fix to one of the three beads above fixes a leaf without
171	addressing the trunk.
172	
173	This ADR exists because the next round of refactors (the `try/` and `work/`
174	packages, the `ExecuteLoopSpec` unification, the stay-alive fix) will all
175	re-cement the current bipartite design unless the architectural shift is
176	agreed first.
177	
178	## Decision
179	
180	**Workers are always autonomous. There is no "--local mode" — the
181	worker has one mode (autonomous) and detects a server continuously. If
182	a server is reachable, the worker emits events and results to it
183	best-effort. If no server is reachable (none running, partition, never
184	configured), the worker proceeds with its work and tries again later.
185	The bead store is the source of truth; the server is an optional
186	observability + coordination target.**
187	
188	Concretely:
189	
190	1. A worker process (`ddx work`) reads the bead store directly, picks
191	   the next eligible bead per the current picker logic (priority asc,
192	   FIFO within tier), claims it atomically, executes via `try.Attempt`,
193	   writes evidence locally, and reports the result by writing the bead's
194	   event log + closing the bead via the store.
195	2. **All of #1 happens regardless of whether a server is running.**
196	   This is the existing autonomous behavior; the worker has no
197	   dependency on a server for correctness.
198	3. **In parallel**, a small "server probe" goroutine inside the worker
199	   periodically (every N seconds, default 30) checks
200	   `~/.local/share/ddx/server.addr` (XDG-compliant, matches current code) for a reachable server. State transitions:
201	   - **NotConnected → Connected:** server appeared; worker POSTs
202	     `register` with a thin identity envelope (project root, harness,
203	     executor pid/host); on success, worker stores the `worker_id` and
204	     starts mirroring events to the server best-effort.
205	   - **Connected → Connected:** server still reachable; continue
206	     mirroring.
207	   - **Connected → NotConnected:** server unreachable (crash,
208	     partition, stopped); worker logs the transition, stops trying to
209	     mirror events, retains its bead-store-local event log, continues
210	     working.
211	   - **NotConnected → NotConnected:** still no server; nothing to do.
212	4. When in Connected state, the worker mirrors bead events + final
213	   results to `/api/workers/<id>/event` and the bead-close result to
214	   the same endpoint best-effort. Failures (server crash mid-POST,
215	   partition, 5xx) are logged locally and ignored — the bead's local
216	   event log is authoritative.
217	5. The server's view (which workers exist, which beads are running,
218	   recent events) is **derived from worker reports** — eventually
219	   consistent, NOT authoritative. The server's value is centralized
220	   observability, cross-worker dashboards, operator UI, and
221	   server-initiated cancel (which writes a marker to the bead store
222	   that workers honor on next iteration).
223	6. Server restart drops the in-memory derived view; on next probe cycle
224	   the worker re-registers (or notices the server is gone). No
225	   in-flight work is at risk because workers don't depend on the
226	   server.
227	
228	**`--local` flag is removed.** Today's `--local` semantics are the
229	default behavior; there's nothing to opt into. Operators who pass
230	`--local` see a deprecation warning + the flag is a no-op (no
231	"force standalone" behavior — the worker probes for a server normally;
232	operators wanting to suppress the probe can stop the server). After one
233	alpha release with the deprecation warning, the flag is deleted. The
234	breaking-changes section documents this.
235	
236	This is **Proposal B-shaped** (server is passive observer) with one
237	addition: the server can write back to the bead store to influence
238	worker decisions (e.g., cancel a bead by setting an extra field; set
239	priority overrides). Workers see those changes on next bead-store read.
240	
241	### Why autonomous-default workers
242	
243	- **Workers don't depend on the server for correctness.** Any server
244	  restart, partition, crash, or just "no server running" is a no-op for
245	  the worker's primary job (executing beads).
246	- **Same code path for every invocation.** `ddx work` and `ddx work
247	  --local` differ only in whether they bother trying to reach a server.
248	  No in-process API server, no Transport interface, no test-mode
249	  fixture — the existing unit-test setup (no server) is the autonomous
250	  case.
251	- **Server is opt-in observability.** Operators who want centralized
252	  dashboards run a server; operators who don't, don't. Either is
253	  correct.
254	- **No claim coordination races.** The bead store's atomic claim is the
255	  only claim primitive. Two workers contending for the same bead use
256	  the same store-level CAS that exists today; there is no parallel
257	  server-side claim table to race against.
258	- **Project boundaries enforced by the existing per-project bead store.**
259	  Each `.ddx/beads.jsonl` is a project; workers operate against one
260	  project's store; the cross-project leak class (`ddx-4c51d33e`) is
261	  prevented at the worker level because workers never read another
262	  project's store.
263	- **`ExecuteLoopSpec` from `ddx-29058e2a` still applies** — but as a
264	  worker-side struct unification (cobra → in-process flow), not a wire
265	  format. The drop class is closed by the same single-struct
266	  discipline; it just doesn't need to cross a network boundary.
267	
268	### Threat model (limits of session-token project binding)
269	
270	Session tokens prevent **honest worker mixup** (a misconfigured worker for
271	project A cannot accidentally claim a bead from project B). They are NOT a
272	defense against a **local malicious process**: `requireTrusted` accepts any
273	loopback connection without identity proof, so any process on the same host
274	can register as any project. The session-token model is project-scoping for
275	legitimate workers, not authentication of worker identity. Future work to
276	strengthen this (e.g., per-worker keypairs, ts-net WhoIs binding for
277	loopback registrations) is out of scope for v1 and tracked separately.
278	
279	### Picker (worker-side, status quo with diagnostic events)
280	
281	The bead picker stays where it lives today: in the worker, against the
282	bead store. The fix at commit `80f51574` added `picker.priority_skip`
283	and `picker.claim_race` diagnostic events; both are preserved. Picker
284	rules (unchanged from current code):
285	
286	1. Read eligible beads via `Store.ReadyExecution()` (excludes superseded,
287	   not-execution-eligible, in retry-cooldown).
288	2. Sort by priority ascending (P0 first), then by `updated_at` ascending
289	   (FIFO within tier).
290	3. If `--label-filter` is set, intersect with bead labels.
291	4. Skip beads in the per-Run `attempted` map (set after each unsuccessful
292	   claim attempt; reset on falling through to "no candidate" per the
293	   stay-alive fix at commit `41cb762e`).
294	5. Claim atomically via `Store.Claim()`; on CAS loss, emit
295	   `picker.claim_race` and continue to next eligible.
296	
297	Two workers contending for the same top bead use the bead store's
298	existing atomic claim — the same primitive that has worked correctly
299	for as long as DDx has had a bead store. There is no parallel server-side
300	claim table to race against.
301	
302	When the server is reachable, the worker mirrors the
303	`picker.priority_skip` and `picker.claim_race` events to the server's
304	ingestion endpoint so operators can see them in the UI. When no server,
305	the events still land in the bead's local event stream.
306	
307	### Why not centralized server-side picker (the rev 1-3 design)
308	
309	- Workers don't need it for correctness — the bead store's atomic claim
310	  is sufficient. Adding a server-side claim table creates a parallel
311	  source of truth that has to be reconciled.
312	- Server-side picker requires workers to depend on the server, which
313	  contradicts the autonomous-default decision.
314	- Diagnostic events from commit `80f51574` already make starvation
315	  observable; centralization didn't add visibility we lacked.
316	
317	### Why not Proposal A (server orchestrates, in-process API for --local)
318	
319	(Earlier revisions chose this; rev 4 reverses.)
320	
321	- The in-process API server for `--local` was complexity in service of a
322	  uniformity that the user explicitly doesn't need. `--local` working
323	  *without* a server is the natural and operator-expected behavior.
324	- "Server is the source of truth for runtime state" requires server
325	  restart recovery, claim reconciliation, partition handling, and a
326	  long-poll back-pressure regime — none of which are needed when the
327	  bead store is the source of truth.
328	- Workers being long-lived clients of a server requires the server to be
329	  available for the worker to function, which the user explicitly
330	  rejected.
331	
332	## Worker-server interface (best-effort reporting)
333	
334	The worker-server boundary is small. There is no HTTP API for claim
335	coordination, no long-poll, no heartbeat lease, no in-process server.
336	There are exactly two endpoints, both used best-effort by workers and
337	both backed by `requireTrusted` (loopback or ts-net per ADR-006):
338	
339	### POST /api/workers/register
340	
341	Worker-side: called by the server-probe goroutine on every
342	NotConnected → Connected transition (NOT just at startup). On
343	re-connect after server crash, the worker re-registers and gets a new
344	`worker_id`. Discovery uses
345	`~/.local/share/ddx/server.addr` (XDG-compliant, matches current code)
346	discovery. Body is a small identity envelope:
347	
348	```json
349	{
350	  "project_root": "/abs/path",
351	  "harness": "claude",
352	  "model": "",
353	  "executor_pid": 12345,
354	  "executor_host": "host.local",
355	  "started_at": "2026-05-03T03:00:00Z"
356	}
357	```
358	
359	Response:
360	
361	```json
362	{ "worker_id": "wkr-9f3a..." }
363	```
364	
365	The worker uses `worker_id` as a correlation key in subsequent event
366	reports. If registration fails (no server, network error, 5xx), the
367	worker proceeds without a `worker_id` and skips event mirroring. No
368	session token, no heartbeat, no claim lease — none of those concepts
369	exist in the worker-server protocol because the worker doesn't depend
370	on the server.
371	
372	### POST /api/workers/<id>/event
373	
374	Worker-side: called best-effort whenever the worker would write an event
375	to a bead's local event log. Body mirrors the bead event:
376	
377	```json
378	{
379	  "bead_id": "ddx-076147ee",
380	  "attempt_id": "20260503T021424-dac100b6",
381	  "kind": "attempt.started" | "picker.priority_skip" | "loop.idle" | "result" | "...",
382	  "body": { "...": "..." }
383	}
384	```
385	
386	Server appends to its derived view (in-memory + optionally backed by
387	its own `.ddx/server/events.jsonl`). Response: 204. Worker's local
388	event log is the authoritative copy; server-side is for observability.
389	
390	If the POST fails for any reason, the worker logs the failure locally
391	and continues. Lost events are tolerable because the bead's local event
392	stream has the full record; operators wanting to backfill can replay
393	from the bead store.
394	
395	### What the server can do (the value-add)
396	
397	Once the server has a derived view of who's reporting:
398	
399	- **Centralized observability**: which workers exist, what they're doing
400	  right now, recent picker decisions, recent dropped commits — all
401	  surfaced via existing GraphQL and the workers panel.
402	- **Cross-worker correlation**: see all workers across all projects in
403	  one place; useful for multi-project hosts.
404	- **Operator UI**: queue beads, cancel beads (via writing markers to the
405	  bead store that workers honor on next iteration), view bead history.
406	- **Server-initiated cancel**: not a direct API call to the worker;
407	  instead, server writes `extra.cancel-requested: true` to the bead;
408	  worker observes on next iteration of its loop and aborts the attempt
409	  at the next safe point.
410	
411	### What the server explicitly does NOT do
412	
413	- It does not assign beads to workers (workers pick from the bead store
414	  themselves).
415	- It does not hold authoritative claim state (the bead store does).
416	- It does not gate worker startup or operation (workers run without it).
417	- It does not hold session tokens or enforce per-worker auth (a future
418	  hardening; v1 trusts the requireTrusted boundary).
419	- It does not need to survive worker death gracefully (workers are
420	  independent; server's derived view is replayable from the bead
421	  stores at any time).
422	
423	### Server crash / restart behavior
424	
425	Server crashes: workers' next event POST fails; workers log + continue.
426	Server restarts: workers' next event POST succeeds (or 410 if the
427	server didn't recover the worker_id, in which case the worker
428	re-registers and resumes). No in-flight work is at risk.
429	
430	### Probe + freshness state model
431	
432	Smaller than rev 3's claim/heartbeat model but still needs a single
433	table. The server-probe goroutine:
434	
435	| Event | Threshold | Action |
436	|---|---|---|
437	| Worker process start | immediate (0s) | Probe `~/.local/share/ddx/server.addr`; on success, register |
438	| Probe interval (steady state) | 30s default; 10s min; 5min max; jittered ±20% | Re-check reachability; emit register if NotConnected→Connected; nothing if Connected→Connected |
439	| Connected POST fails (timeout, conn refused, 5xx) | immediate | Worker enters NotConnected; logs locally; continues working |
440	| 5 consecutive probe failures | ~2.5 min default | Worker reduces probe rate to 5min (backoff); resets to 30s on next success |
441	| Server replies 410 unknown_worker | immediate | Worker re-registers within same probe cycle |
442	| Worker process exit (graceful) | immediate | Best-effort POST disconnect; not required for correctness |
443	
444	**Freshness indicators** (server-side, surfaced in the workers panel UI):
445	
446	- `worker_state` ∈ {connected, stale, disconnected}: connected = event
447	  in last 2× probe interval; stale = 2-10× probe interval; disconnected
448	  = > 10× probe interval (worker presumed dead)
449	- `last_event_at`: timestamp of most recent event from this worker
450	- `mirror_failures_count`: total POST failures since worker_id was issued
451	  (operator can see if a worker is healthy but lossy)
452	
453	### Disconnected-work backfill
454	
455	A worker that started while no server was running may have claimed and
456	finished beads before reaching Connected. To prevent the operator's UI
457	from missing those events:
458	
459	- The worker maintains an in-memory ring buffer of the last N=200
460	  events it emitted while NotConnected (kind, body, bead_id,
461	  attempt_id, timestamp).
462	- On NotConnected → Connected transition, the worker POSTs a
463	  `/api/workers/<id>/backfill` request with the buffered events. Server
464	  acknowledges with 204; worker clears the buffer.
465	- If the buffer overflows (worker generated >200 events while
466	  disconnected — possible during a long outage or many short attempts),
467	  oldest events are dropped silently. The bead's local event log
468	  remains complete; only the server's derived view is incomplete. UI
469	  marks the worker as `had_dropped_backfill: true` so operators know to
470	  consult bead-local logs.
471	- Backfill is best-effort: a 5xx during backfill leaves the buffer
472	  intact; worker tries again on next NotConnected → Connected.
473	
474	### Cancel SLA
475	
476	Operator-initiated cancel writes `extra.cancel-requested: true` to the
477	bead via the server's `/api/beads/<id>/cancel` endpoint (or directly
478	via the bead store). The worker honors at the next safe point:
479	
480	- **Mid-attempt poll** (default every 10s during long attempts): the
481	  worker re-reads the bead's `extra` map. If `cancel-requested: true`
482	  appears, the worker aborts the next LLM turn / next git operation
483	  boundary and reports `preserved_for_review` with reason `operator_cancel`.
484	- **Worst-case latency**: 10s (mid-attempt poll interval) plus the
485	  current LLM turn duration (typically 5-30s). Operators expecting
486	  faster cancel use OS signals.
487	- **Idempotency**: a bead with `cancel-requested: true` already worked
488	  is silently consumed (worker writes `cancel-honored: true` next to
489	  it); a worker starting work on a bead with `cancel-requested: true`
490	  immediately reports `preserved_for_review` and skips the attempt.
491	
492	## Compatibility analysis
493	
494	### Migrates cleanly (almost everything)
495	
496	- **Existing autonomous worker behavior.** This is the baseline rev 4
497	  preserves. Today's `ddx work` and `ddx work --local` already work
498	  against the bead store; rev 4 is additive (best-effort server
499	  reporting on top of unchanged behavior).
500	- **Bead event log shape.** Worker events go to the bead's local event
501	  log (today's behavior); the server-side ingestion endpoint receives a
502	  copy with the same shape. No reader changes.
503	- **Evidence layout.** `.ddx/executions/<run-id>/` unchanged.
504	- **`try.Attempt` semantics.** Unchanged. The worker calls into `try`
505	  exactly as today.
506	- **`requireTrusted` boundary.** Reused for the two new endpoints; no
507	  new auth plane.
508	- **Existing per-Run `attempted` map and stay-alive defaults.** Preserved
509	  from commit `41cb762e`. The autonomous picker is the picker.
510	
511	### Breaking changes for operators
512	
513	- **None for the autonomous path.** `ddx work` and `ddx work --local`
514	  behave identically to today (with the addition of best-effort server
515	  reporting when a server is reachable).
516	- **Some legacy server-spawn integration tests will need updating.** The
517	  current server-spawn path that hand-marshals `ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec`
518	  over an internal contract is replaced by the server `exec`'ing
519	  `ddx work` like an operator would. Tests asserting specific spawn
520	  args will need to assert the new exec form. (No operator-visible
521	  behavior change here — operators don't call the legacy endpoint
522	  directly.)
523	- **Per-user direction**: the previous `ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec` path
524	  through the server is removed; no compat shim and no data migration
525	  for legacy `.ddx/workers/` records (delete history if needed).
526	
527	### Tests that need updating (smaller list than rev 1-3)
528	
529	- `cli/internal/server/workers_test.go` — exec-spawn tests: assert the
530	  new exec form (`ddx work` invocation), not the JSON request/response
531	  envelope.
532	- Integration tests asserting specific `.ddx/workers/` on-disk format
533	  may need adjustment if rev 4 changes that layout. Currently rev 4 does
534	  NOT modify `.ddx/workers/` shape; workers continue to write spec.json
535	  + status.json there for `ddx agent doctor` consumption.
536	- New tests for the two endpoints: `TestWorkerRegister_HappyPath`,
537	  `TestWorkerRegister_ServerDown_WorkerProceeds`,
538	  `TestWorkerEvent_Mirrored_BestEffort`,
539	  `TestWorkerEvent_ServerCrash_WorkerLogs_Continues`.
540	
541	The tests-that-break list is dramatically smaller than rev 1-3 because
542	the worker state machine, bead store interaction, and evidence layout
543	are all unchanged. Only the new server-side ingestion endpoint and its
544	two tests are net-new.
545	
546	## Sequencing relative to in-flight work
547	
548	Rev 4 dramatically simplifies sequencing. The architecture is additive:
549	the worker keeps working as it does today; server-side reporting is new
550	machinery that sits alongside. There is no "freeze C5/C7/C9 until X"
551	rule because nothing about the worker state machine changes.
552	
553	- **`ddx-29058e2a` (ExecuteLoopSpec unification)** — independent. Rev 4
554	  does NOT make ExecuteLoopSpec a wire format. The bead remains valuable
555	  as a worker-side struct unification (cobra → in-process flow) and
556	  should land independently.
557	- **`ddx-5cb6e6cd` C5 (no_changes adjudication)** — NOT FROZEN. Rev 5
558	  doesn't reshape no_changes generation; mirroring stays an observer.
559	- **`ddx-5cb6e6cd` C7 (uniform Guard contract; delete attempted/hookFailed maps)** — **COORDINATE.** Direct conflict: rev 5's worker keeps the per-Run `attempted` map (preserved from commit `41cb762e`); C7 wants to delete it. Resolution: C7 lands BEFORE rev 5 implementation work, OR rev 5 implementation work coordinates with C7's replacement (Guard-derived exclusion). Until coordinated, C7 stays parked.
560	- **`ddx-5cb6e6cd` C9 (StopCondition + cost-cap)** — NOT FROZEN. The autonomous worker keeps the stay-alive defaults; C9's StopCondition becomes an internal worker construct, server sees terminal state via the result event.
561	- **`ddx-dc157075` (stay-alive at commit `41cb762e`)** — already shipped
562	  and preserved. Worker's autonomous behavior plus 30s poll-interval
563	  default carry forward unchanged.
564	- **`ddx-4c51d33e` (cross-project leak at commit `33b97f25`)** — already
565	  shipped. Rev 4 prevents recurrence at the worker level (one worker
566	  per project bead store; no cross-store reads).
567	- **`ddx-9d55601f` (picker priority bug at commit `80f51574`)** — picker
568	  diagnostic events stay; rev 4 explicitly mirrors them to the server
569	  endpoint when the worker is Connected.
570	- **`ddx-9e4c238d` (auto-routing rejection bug, just filed)** —
571	  independent. Fixing DDx's pre-flight to pass through to fizeau is
572	  orthogonal to the autonomous-default architecture. Should land in
573	  parallel.
574	- **`ddx-1d867ec1` (rename `execute_bead_loop.go`)** — coordinate. The
575	  rename should align with whatever package layout the worker uses
576	  post-rev-4. Probably stays as `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go`
577	  for now since rev 4 doesn't reshape the worker package; revisit when
578	  C13 lands.
579	- **`ddx-076147ee` (this TD)** — closes when the rev 4 ADR is approved
580	  and the implementation roadmap below is filed as beads.
581	- **`ddx-50da9674` (clean fixture repo)** — supports rev 4's acceptance
582	  test; independent.
583	
584	## Implementation roadmap
585	
586	Rev 5 expands from rev 4's 5 beads to 9, after codex flagged missing
587	UI/doctor/FEAT-006/server-spawn-migration/backfill scope.
588	
589	1. **server: ingestion endpoints** — implement `POST /api/workers/register` + `POST /api/workers/<id>/event` + `POST /api/workers/<id>/backfill`; in-memory derived view backed by append-only `.ddx/server/worker-events.jsonl`; freshness state machine (connected/stale/disconnected); requireTrusted boundary. ~300 LOC.
590	
591	2. **worker: server-probe goroutine + best-effort event mirror** — periodic reachability check on `~/.local/share/ddx/server.addr` (XDG-compliant, matches current code) with jittered 30s interval, immediate first probe, exponential backoff on consecutive failures; transitions emit register/disconnect; in-memory ring buffer for backfill (200-event cap); event POSTs are best-effort. ~250 LOC.
592	
593	3. **server: operator-cancel** — `/api/beads/<id>/cancel` writes `extra.cancel-requested: true`; worker mid-attempt poll (10s default) checks bead `extra` and aborts at next safe point with `preserved_for_review` reason `operator_cancel`; idempotency via `cancel-honored: true`. ~150 LOC.
594	
595	4. **CLI: deprecate `--local`** — flag becomes no-op with deprecation warning; update CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, getting-started, and the cobra help text. Existing tests asserting `--local`-specific behavior get rewritten or deleted. (Codex notes the sweep is larger than 50 LOC: `cli/cmd/work_test.go`, `agent_execute_loop_test.go`, `zero_config_work_test.go`, `skills/ddx/reference/work.md`, demos.) ~150 LOC + doc edits.
596	
597	5. **server: derived-view GraphQL + UI workers panel** — schema fields: `workers { id, project, harness, state, last_event_at, mirror_failures_count, had_dropped_backfill, current_bead, current_attempt }`; UI surfaces freshness indicators + duplicate-worker display + the "trusted-peer reported, not authoritative" labeling. Existing workers panel migrated. ~400 LOC frontend + ~100 LOC backend.
598	
599	6. **`ddx agent doctor` migration** — read worker state from server's runtime registry when available; fall back to `.ddx/workers/` on-disk files when no server. The on-disk format stays as the fallback-source-of-truth for one alpha release lag, then deprecated. ~150 LOC.
600	
601	7. **server-spawn path migration** — `cli/internal/server/workers.go` and `handleStartExecuteLoopWorker` switch from hand-marshalled spec to `exec ddx work` with env-vars. The legacy spec serialization deletes when this lands. ~200 LOC + test updates.
602	
603	8. **FEAT-006 amendment** — replace the rev 3 worker-contract section with rev 5's autonomous-default + best-effort-mirror description. Drop session-token/heartbeat/`next-bead`/result/disconnect contract; add register/event/backfill/cancel + freshness state. ~200 lines docs.
604	
605	9. **acceptance + soak** — uses `ddx-50da9674` clean fixture repo. Multi-worker drain WITH server; restart server mid-flight; verify worker continues + reconnects + backfills. Then drain with NO server; verify identical behavior. Operator-cancel test (write marker, observe `preserved_for_review` with `operator_cancel` reason within SLA). Soak: ≥1 week of normal operator use. Final bead.
606	
607	Plus one COORDINATION bead, NOT new work but a parking-lot for the
608	C7 conflict:
609	
610	10. **coordinate: C7 (Guard contract, delete attempted map) with rev 5 worker** — either C7 lands first and rev 5 implementation uses Guard-derived exclusion, OR C7 stays parked until rev 5's worker code defines its replacement for the per-Run attempted map. Decision documented; no LOC.
611	
612	Total estimated effort: 2-4 weeks (vs 1-2 weeks for rev 4's understated 5-bead roadmap, vs 8-10 weeks for rev 3's design).
613	
614	## Consequences
615	
616	- **Workers don't depend on the server.** The headline operator
617	  requirement (server restart preserves in-flight work) is satisfied
618	  by construction: workers don't notice server restarts as anything
619	  more than transient mirror failures.
620	- **No `--local` flag.** Removed from the CLI surface. Behavior of
621	  today's `--local` becomes the always-on default. Backward compat
622	  preserved for one release via a no-op flag with deprecation warning.
623	- **Server is opt-in observability.** Operators who don't run a server
624	  see no behavior change from today. Operators who do see
625	  centralized event aggregation, cross-worker visibility, and an
626	  operator UI. Both are correct configurations.
627	- **`ExecuteLoopSpec` unification still happens.** Per `ddx-29058e2a` —
628	  the spec is a worker-side struct, not a wire format. The drop class
629	  is closed by single-struct discipline; it just doesn't need to cross
630	  a network boundary.
631	- **No claim coordination races.** Bead store's atomic claim is the
632	  only claim primitive. Two workers contending use the same store
633	  CAS that has worked correctly for as long as DDx has had a bead
634	  store.
635	- **No "kill server → workers exit" tests left valid.** Any test that
636	  relied on this behavior gets updated to assert "kill server →
637	  workers continue working autonomously."
638	- **Server restart loses derived view briefly.** The server's view of
639	  who's working what comes back within one probe cycle (default 30s)
640	  as workers re-register. No in-flight work is at risk.
641	- **Operator UX for cancel changes slightly.** Today operators kill
642	  worker subprocesses or wait. After: operators send cancel via the
643	  server (or write the marker to the bead store directly); worker
644	  honors at next safe point. Documented in CHANGELOG.
645	- **Evidence layout unchanged.** `.ddx/executions/<run-id>/` still
646	  receives all per-attempt artifacts; worker writes there exactly as
647	  today.
648	
649	## References
650	
651	- Bead `ddx-076147ee` — this TD's source.
652	- Bead `ddx-29058e2a` — `ExecuteLoopSpec` drift; rev 5 keeps this as a worker-side struct unification (NOT a wire format).
653	- Bead `ddx-5cb6e6cd` — `run`/`try`/`work` refactor epic; rev 5 sequences C5/C9 in parallel, C7 coordinated.
654	- Bead `ddx-dc157075` — stay-alive fix at commit `41cb762e`; rev 5 keeps the autonomous-loop semantics.
655	- Bead `ddx-4c51d33e` — cross-project leak (LAYER 1 at commit `33b97f25`); rev 5 prevents recurrence at the worker level (one worker per project bead store).
656	- Bead `ddx-9d55601f` — picker priority bug at commit `80f51574`; diagnostic events mirror to server when Connected.
657	- Bead `ddx-9e4c238d` — auto-routing rejection bug; orthogonal to rev 5.
658	- Bead `ddx-50da9674` — clean fixture repo; rev 5's acceptance soak depends on it.
659	- ADR-006 — ts-net authentication (trust model for `requireTrusted`).
660	- ADR-007 — federation topology (multi-node ownership of project queues).
661	- ADR-021 — operator-prompt beads (existing trust pattern this ADR mirrors).
662	- ADR-004 — bead-backed runtime storage (durable substrate beneath the runtime registry).
663	- FEAT-006 — agent service (this ADR adds a "Worker contract" section there).
664	- FEAT-002 — server (the API surface this ADR extends).
665	- FEAT-010 — executions (the per-attempt evidence layout the worker writes).
666	"}}

  <governing>
    <ref id="SD-024" path="docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-024-config-driven-runtime-options.md">Solution Design: Config-Driven Runtime Options</ref>
  </governing>
  <instructions>
You are executing one bead inside an isolated DDx execution worktree. The bead's <description> and <acceptance> sections above are the completion contract — every AC checkbox must be provably satisfied by a specific piece of code, test, or file you can point to after your commit.

## How to work

1. Read first. If the bead description names files, read them to see what is already there. If the bead references a spec, ADR, or API contract, read the relevant sections before writing code. Do not start editing until you understand what the bead wants changed and why.

2. Cross-reference your work against the acceptance criteria as you go. Before committing, walk through the AC one item at a time and identify the specific test name, file path, or function that satisfies each checkbox. If you cannot point to concrete evidence for an AC item, it is not done.

3. Run the project's verification commands before committing. Use whatever commands match the crate or package you touched — typically `cargo test` and `cargo clippy` for Rust, `bun test` or `npm test` for JS/TS, `go test` for Go. The bead description usually names the exact commands. **Do not commit red code.** If a test or lint fails, fix it first.

4. Commit once, when everything is green. Stage only the files you intentionally changed with `git add <specific-paths>` — never `git add -A`, because there may be unrelated WIP in this worktree. Use a conventional-commit-style subject ending with `[<bead-id>]`. DDx will merge your commits back to the base branch.

5. If you cannot complete the bead in this pass, write your reasoning to `.ddx/executions/20260427T000819-3d343cfa/no_changes_rationale.txt` with: (a) what is done, (b) what is blocking, (c) what a follow-up attempt would need. Do NOT commit partial, exploratory, or red code hoping the reviewer will accept it — a well-justified no_changes is better than a bad commit. The bead will be re-queued for another attempt, potentially with a stronger model.

## The bead contract overrides project defaults

The bead description and AC override any CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or project-level conservative defaults in this worktree. If the bead asks for new documentation, write it. If the bead adds a new module or crate, add it. Conservative rules (YAGNI, DOWITYTD, no-docs-unless-asked) do not apply inside execute-bead — the bead IS the ask.

## Quality bar and the review step

After your commit merges, an automated review step may check your work against the acceptance criteria. If any AC item is unmet, the bead reopens and escalates to a higher-capability model, and the review findings are threaded into the next attempt's prompt as a `<review-findings>` section. **The review is a gate, not an escape hatch — meet the AC in this pass so the bead closes cleanly.**

If this prompt already contains a `<review-findings>` section, address every BLOCKING finding before claiming the work complete. Those findings are the precise list of what the previous attempt missed.

## Constraints

- Work only inside this execution worktree.
- Keep `.ddx/executions/` intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- **Never run `ddx init`** — the workspace is already initialized. Running it corrupts the bead queue and project configuration.
- Do not modify files outside the scope the bead description names.
- Do not rewrite CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or any other project-instructions file unless the bead explicitly asks for it.

## When the work is done

After the commit succeeds and you have verified every AC item, stop. Return control to the orchestrator. Do not continue to explore the repository, run extra tests, or generate follow-up notes — the bead is complete, and returning promptly is how execute-bead signals success.
  </instructions>
</execute-bead>

418		// DefaultTimeoutMS is the default idle (inactivity) timeout — resets on
419		// every stdout/stderr byte or agent event. 2 hours is long enough for any
420		// task where the provider streams progress; a stuck provider still needs
421		// DefaultWallClockMS to guarantee termination.
422		DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000 // 2 hours
423		// DefaultWallClockMS is the absolute wall-clock cap applied in addition to
424		// the idle timeout. It fires regardless of stream/event activity, so a
425		// provider that emits heartbeats forever cannot pin a worker indefinitely.
426		// Sized at 3h — roughly 1.5x the idle timeout — so normal long tasks still
427		// complete while genuinely hung workers free themselves. See
428		// RC2 of ddx-0a651925 for the incident that motivated this bound.
429		DefaultWallClockMS = 10800000 // 3 hours
430		DefaultLogDir      = ".ddx/agent-logs"
431	)
432	
433	// ResolveLogDir returns an absolute session-log directory path anchored at
434	// projectRoot. Callers that construct an agent.Runner must use this to avoid
435	// the Runner's relative DefaultLogDir resolving against process CWD — which
436	// historically wrote logs to stray locations like cli/internal/server/.ddx/.
437	func ResolveLogDir(projectRoot, configured string) string {
438		if configured == "" {
439			configured = DefaultLogDir
440		}
441		if filepath.IsAbs(configured) {
442			return configured
443		}
444		if projectRoot == "" {
445			return configured
446		}
447		return filepath.Join(projectRoot, configured)
448	}
449	"}}

    <title>bead: scaffold cli/internal/bead/axon genqlient client and subscriptions</title>
    <description>
PROBLEM
The Axon backend contract requires a generated GraphQL client plus a WebSocket subscription client, but the current implementation is an in-process JSONL emulation instead of a typed Axon package. The gap is visible in cli/internal/bead/axon_backend.go:15-26, which says the backend is a local emulation and not the wire-path implementation. There is no cli/internal/bead/axon/ package yet.

ROOT CAUSE
cli/internal/bead/axon_backend.go:20-26 explicitly keeps the backend as a JSONL-shaped emulation, so there is nowhere for genqlient-generated bindings, schema snapshots, query documents, or a subscription transport to live. The repo has no Axon client package to satisfy TD-030's GraphQL-only requirement.

PROPOSED FIX
- Create cli/internal/bead/axon/schema.graphql with the pinned introspection snapshot.
- Add query/mutation/subscription documents under cli/internal/bead/axon/queries/.
- Add genqlient config and generated Go bindings under cli/internal/bead/axon/.
- Add a small subscription transport in the same package for GraphQL WS changeEvents.

NON-SCOPE
- Replacing backend_axon.go itself; that is the follow-up implementation bead.
- Config routing and package-level test matrix changes.
- Axon server-side schema work.
    </description>

3. cd cli && go test ./internal/bead/axon/... passes.
4. lefthook run pre-commit passes.

  <instructions>
You are executing one bead inside an isolated DDx execution worktree. The bead's <description> and <acceptance> are the completion contract — every AC must be provably satisfied by a specific code, test, or file you can point to after your commit.

## Step 0: size check

The bead is too big when any of these holds:

- More than ~6 ACs spanning unrelated subsystems.
- AC mixes design, implementation, integration tests, and docs as separate top-level deliverables.
- Description names multiple feature-sized sub-pieces.
- More than ~500 lines across more than ~5 files in unrelated packages.

If too big, decompose — do not attempt the work:

1. `ddx bead create` for each child slice (focused title, description, AC; copy parent's labels and spec-id).
2. `ddx bead dep add <child-id> <parent-id>` to record edges.
3. `ddx bead update <parent-id> --notes 'decomposed into <child-ids>'` so the decomposition is visible.
4. Write `.ddx/executions/20260505T024017-db84a9cd/no_changes_rationale.txt` listing each child ID and slice, then stop.

A clean decomposition is a successful attempt. Do not mix partial implementation with decomposition.

## How to work

- Read first. If the bead names files, specs, or prior beads, read them before editing — do not guess.
- Cross-reference each AC against concrete evidence (test name, file path, function) before committing. If you cannot point at it, it is not done.
- Run the project's test and lint commands before committing. **Do not commit red code** — fix failures first.
- Stage with `git add <specific-paths>`; never `git add -A` (the worktree may have unrelated WIP).
- Commit exactly once when green; conventional-commit subject ending with `[<bead-id>]`. Stop after the commit.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's scope.
- If you cannot finish, write `.ddx/executions/20260505T024017-db84a9cd/no_changes_rationale.txt` (what is done, what blocks, what a follow-up needs) before exiting. No commit and no rationale ⇒ DDx records `no_evidence_produced`. A well-justified no_changes beats a bad commit.

## no_changes contract

If you write `.ddx/executions/20260505T024017-db84a9cd/no_changes_rationale.txt`, it MUST carry one of:

- `verification_command: <one-line shell command>` — the loop runs it (cwd = project root, 60s); exit 0 closes the bead, non-zero rejects.
- `status: needs_investigation` + `reason: <why>` — bead stays open for triage.

Bare rationales ("nothing to do") are rejected.

## Reports

Freestanding artifacts (investigation reports, findings docs) go under `.ddx/executions/20260505T024017-db84a9cd/` (the per-attempt evidence directory under `.ddx/executions/`). **Never write reports to `/tmp` or any path outside the repository** — out-of-repo paths are invisible to the post-merge reviewer and cause BLOCK on missing evidence. If the bead names a specific in-repo path, use it; else default to `.ddx/executions/20260505T024017-db84a9cd/<short-name>.md`. Stage and commit the report alongside code.

## Bead overrides project defaults

The bead description and AC override CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and conservative project defaults (YAGNI, DOWITYTD, no-docs-unless-asked) in this worktree — write whatever the bead asks for.

## Review gate

- The review is a gate, not an escape hatch — meet every AC in this pass.
- Address every BLOCKING `<review-findings>` item; do not declare `no_changes` with blocking findings open.

## Constraints

- Work only inside this execution worktree.
- Keep `.ddx/executions/` intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- **Never run `ddx init`** — the workspace is initialized; running it corrupts the bead queue.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's named scope.
- Do not rewrite CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or other project-instructions files unless the bead asks.

## When the work is done

After the commit succeeds and every AC is verified, stop. Return control to the orchestrator — do not keep exploring or testing.

Some provider/model combos don't implement JSON-mode / response_format=json_object / tool-use-required. If a bead prompt depends on structured output, today routing's blindness means the failure surfaces at dispatch.

Per the parent bead's analysis, this is probably covered incidentally by the tool-calling gating sub-bead — most reasoning-tool-capable models also support structured output. This bead exists to either (a) explicitly add a SupportsStructuredOutput capability, or (b) close as 'covered by tool-calling gating' once that bead lands and we verify the assumption.

## Decision gate (do this first)

Before writing code, verify the 'covered by tool-calling' hypothesis:
- Survey the providers we currently route to (anthropic, openai, openrouter, omlx, lmstudio, ollama) and check whether each one's tool-calling support implies JSON-mode support.
- If yes everywhere we care about: close this bead with a note in the tool-calling bead's tests / docs that asserts the implication.
- If no (any counter-example): proceed with scope below.

## Scope (only if decision gate fails)

- Add SupportsStructuredOutput tri-state to ModelEntry / Surface and to capabilities.go's curated table.
- RouteRequest.RequiresStructuredOutput bool; execute-bead sets it when the prompt requires JSON mode.
- evaluateCandidate rejects RequiresStructuredOutput=true on SupportsStructuredOutput=no. Unknown passes with -v warning.

## Files (only if decision gate fails)

- cli/internal/agent/types.go
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go
- cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-22cadb21","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:agent","area:routing"],"notes":"Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing","parent":"ddx-4817edfd","priority":4,"schema_version":1,"status":"closed","title":"routing: structured-output (JSON-mode) gating — likely incidental to tool-calling","updated_at":"2026-04-18T22:43:47.191941677Z"}

Some omlx flavors and older local OpenAI-compat servers do not implement /v1/chat/completions with tools. Today routing fuzzy-matches them and dispatch fails with 'tools not supported' — or worse, silently returns a no-tool response that the execute-bead loop cannot consume.

## Scope

- Per-provider / per-flavor capability metadata: SupportsTools bool. Two layers:
  1. Hand-curated table for known harnesses (claude, codex, opus, etc.) and provider flavors (openai, anthropic, openrouter, omlx, lmstudio, ollama).
  2. Discovery-driven enrichment when /v1/models/status (or equivalent) advertises tool support per model.
- RouteRequest gains RequiresTools bool (default false to preserve current behavior; execute-bead sets true).
- evaluateCandidate rejects candidates where RequiresTools=true and the resolved (provider, flavor, model) combo is known NOT to support tools. Unknown = pass with -v warning, same pattern as context-window bead.
- Capability metadata location: cli/internal/agent/catalog.go on ModelEntry/Surface, plus a curated table in cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go (or co-located).

## Out of scope

- Context-window, structured-output, effort, permissions gating (sibling beads).
- Probing servers actively at routing time (uses cached discovery + table).

## Files

- cli/internal/agent/types.go (RouteRequest.RequiresTools, capability fields on CandidatePlan)
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go (rejection in rejectIfNotViable / evaluateCandidate)
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go (SupportsTools on ModelEntry / Surface)
- cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go (NEW — curated table)","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-854cb8a7","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:agent","area:routing","area:catalog"],"notes":"Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing","parent":"ddx-4817edfd","priority":2,"schema_version":1,"status":"closed","title":"routing: tool-calling support gating with per-flavor capability metadata","updated_at":"2026-04-18T22:43:47.285105492Z"}

5. go test ./cli/... is green.","archived_at":"2026-05-07T04:29:14Z","closing_commit_sha":"5328120be55ab6af07a5cd35c7dc7f7a22f30f96","created_at":"2026-04-18T06:50:41.86041297Z","description":"## Provider-specific honoring of --effort and --permissions (sibling of ddx-4817edfd).

Today, Harness.EffortFlag is checked at routing.go:rejectIfNotViable, so harnesses without effort support are rejected when --effort is set. But provider-level gating is missing: e.g., openrouter doesn't honor --effort on non-reasoning models, and --permissions unrestricted may not apply to every harness+provider combination. Routing should reject or downgrade rather than dispatch and silently no-op the flag.

## Scope

- Add effort/permissions capability fields to the curated capability table (same file added by the tool-calling sub-bead, or co-locate here if that one ships first).
  - SupportsEffort tri-state per (provider, model).
  - SupportsPermission(permission_class) — at minimum 'unrestricted'.
- evaluateCandidate / rejectIfNotViable: when RouteRequest names an --effort or --permissions value not honored by the resolved (provider, model) combo, reject with a clear reason. Unknown = pass with -v warning.
- Document expected behavior for each known provider in capabilities.go comments (openrouter non-reasoning models = no effort, etc.).

## Out of scope

- Context-window, tool-calling, structured-output gating (sibling beads).
- Building a 'downgrade' path (e.g., silently strip --effort and continue). Rejection is sufficient for v1; downgrade is a follow-up.

## Files

- cli/internal/agent/routing.go
- cli/internal/agent/capabilities.go (shared with tool-calling bead — or new file if order differs)
- cli/internal/agent/types.go","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-bb230ad5","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:agent","area:routing"],"notes":"Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing","parent":"ddx-4817edfd","priority":3,"schema_version":1,"status":"closed","title":"routing: effort/permissions gating per provider+model (not just per harness)","updated_at":"2026-04-18T22:43:47.3806568Z"}

3. Unknown context window does NOT cause rejection; logs a warning at -v.

6. go test ./cli/... is green.","archived_at":"2026-05-07T04:29:14Z","closing_commit_sha":"5328120be55ab6af07a5cd35c7dc7f7a22f30f96","created_at":"2026-04-18T06:50:16.477597182Z","description":"## Smallest useful slice of capability gating (sibling of ddx-4817edfd).

Routing currently does not check whether the resolved model's context window can hold the bead prompt. A 32k-context fuzzy match against an 80k-token bead prompt routes 'successfully' and fails mid-dispatch with a provider 400 or truncated output.

## Scope

- Add optional EstimatedPromptTokens (uint32) to RouteRequest (cli/internal/agent/types.go).
- In evaluateCandidate (cli/internal/agent/routing.go), when EstimatedPromptTokens is set and > 0, reject (or penalize) candidates whose concrete_model context window is below it.
- Source of context window per candidate: v0.3.11's LookupModelLimits first, then catalog ModelEntry.ContextWindow (cli/internal/agent/catalog.go) as fallback. If neither is known, treat as 'unknown' — do NOT reject (avoid false negatives), but log at -v.
- Caller side: pass EstimatedPromptTokens from execute-bead when the bead prompt is assembled (best-effort — can use char/4 heuristic if a tokenizer is unavailable).

## Out of scope

- Tool-calling, structured-output, effort, permissions gating (separate sibling beads).
- Adding a tokenizer dependency. char/4 estimate is fine for v1.

## Files

- cli/internal/agent/types.go
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go
- cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go (or wherever the bead prompt is assembled)","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-f5cec42a","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:agent","area:routing"],"notes":"Superseded by agent-1a486c2e — single routing engine in agent/internal/routing","parent":"ddx-4817edfd","priority":2,"schema_version":1,"status":"closed","title":"routing: context-window gating in evaluateCandidate","updated_at":"2026-04-18T22:43:47.481306052Z"}

The package-level conformance coverage still needs a single matrix that proves both backends satisfy the same behavioral contract. The current chaos harness already names jsonl and axon in cli/internal/bead/chaos_test.go:23-36, but the rest of the package still relies on ad hoc store construction and has no package-wide backend matrix to prove the new Axon path behaves identically.

ROOT CAUSE
cli/internal/bead/chaos_test.go:23-36 only defines the backend table; there is no end-to-end package test matrix yet that runs the conformance suite through both backends and keeps the existing package tests honest. The rest of the test package still defaults to newTestStore(t) in many places, so the Axon path is not yet a first-class test target.

PROPOSED FIX
- Extend the backend test helpers so the package conformance suite can run under both jsonl and axon.
- Keep TestChaos_ConcurrentAppendSafety, TestChaos_AtomicStatusTransitions, TestChaos_ConcurrentCloseAndAppend, and TestChaos_ConcurrentCloseNotLost as explicit backend matrix coverage.
- Make the package test harness clearly fail if either backend diverges.

NON-SCOPE
- GraphQL client generation.
- Backend selection/config plumbing.
- Subscription integration smoke tests.","id":".execute-bead-wt-ddx-8d747049-20260505T023636-7669921a-1a576b9d","issue_type":"task","labels":["phase:2"," area:beads"," area:storage"," kind:feature"," blocked-on-upstream:axon-05c1019d"," blocked-on-upstream:axon-82b6f7b2"],"notes":"Superseded by ddx-958b8fc3.
